channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Administration Seeks to Remove Cuba’s President From Power During Negotiations
item.description: The United States has told Cuba that for meaningful progress to be made in negotiations, President Miguel Díaz-Canel must step down, said people familiar with the talks.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles, Edward Wong and Annie Correal
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba during a visit to Venezuela last year.

item.title: Cuba Ready to Accept Outside Investment, Top Official Says
item.description: President Trump’s words came amid a nationwide blackout and as a top Cuban official said his country would move to open the economy to foreign investors.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Jack Nicas and Frances Robles
item.media:description: The Güinera neighborhood of Havana on Friday.

item.title: Desperation in Cuba Ignites Unusual Acts of Defiance
item.description: A protest in the city of Morón in central Cuba culminated in fiery vandalism at the local Communist Party headquarters.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega

item.title: Oil Prices May Not Fall Soon, U.S. Energy Secretary Says
item.description: Energy Secretary Chris Wright said the Strait of Hormuz, a critical passageway for oil shipments, remained unsafe for tankers. Iran has been firing projectiles and laying mines.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: In an interview with ABC News on Sunday, Energy Secretary Chris Wright expressed some confidence that the United States would be able to secure the Strait of Hormuz within weeks.

item.title: Nordic Leaders Praise Carney as They Discuss Arctic Security
item.description: Canada’s prime minister got a warm welcome from leaders still smarting about President Trump’s remarks two months ago about taking over Greenland.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Thomas Fure/NTB, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada attended a meeting of the leaders of the five Nordic countries on Sunday in Norway.

item.title: Two Arrested in Death of Anti-Iranian Regime Activist in Canada
item.description: A man and a woman were charged with murder in what the authorities described as a “targeted incident” against Masood Masjoody, who had gone missing weeks ago in British Columbia.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.category: Iran Front for the Revival of Law and National Sovereignty

item.title: Some Olympic Leaders Want to See Fixed Winter Games Host Cities
item.description: Fewer cities are bidding for Olympics, and those that are can’t always accommodate every event. The Milan-Cortina Games were the most sprawling Olympics in history.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:description: The International Olympic Committee has had to pivot toward flexibility as the number of bidders for its marquee winter event has started to dwindle.

item.title: Canada Takes Its Sovereignty Push to Space
item.description: The country aims to expand domestic satellite systems and cut dependence on American networks like Elon Musk’s Starlink.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Pat Kane for The New York Times
item.media:description: The northern lights over Yellowknife, Northwest Territories. Scientists worry that space is becoming cluttered with satellites and debris.

item.title: Is Latin America Ready to Abandon Cuba?
item.description: Latin America’s left saw Cuba as its lodestar. Now leaders across the spectrum are hesitant to aid a nation in the Trump administration’s cross hairs.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:description: The Capitolio Nacional in Havana. Cuba’s isolation is deepening in the region.

item.title: Trump and Rubio’s Vision of War: The Art of Destroy and Deal
item.description: Unleashing his inner hawk, Secretary of State Marco Rubio plans President Trump’s military interventions. So far, they favor regime compliance, not change.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio is at the helm of President Trump’s aggressive campaigns to reshape the governments of Iran, Venezuela, Cuba and beyond.

item.title: Cuba’s History Since the Revolution, in Photos
item.description: The Cuban government, which has so far outlasted 13 U.S. presidents, faces its gravest challenge yet. Images from The New York Times and others record nearly seven decades of political turmoil, economic crises and small moments of ordinary life.
item.dc:creator: Craig Allen and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: Old Havana, the capital’s historic center, is a warren of faded facades and sunlit plazas.

item.title: Pentagon’s Boat Bombings Are Illegal, Human Rights Panel Is Told
item.description: Experts condemned the U.S. campaign at a human rights meeting in Guatemala, and the Trump administration condemned the meeting.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
United States Defense and Military Forces
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Jamil Dakwar of the A.C.L.U. said the Pentagon’s attacks were being undertaken without the authorization of Congress and were “in violation of international law on the use of force.”

item.title: Messi Meets Trump and Argentina Is Divided
item.description: Lionel Messi, the Argentine soccer star, accompanied his team, Inter Miami, to the White House. The event turned into political dynamite back in Argentina.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Daniel Politi
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Lionel Messi and other members of Inter Miami in Washington last week.

item.title: Cuba Acknowledges Talks with Trump Officials For the First Time
item.description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel, whose country is rapidly running out of fuel, said the talks were based on “respect for the political systems of both countries.”
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles, Patricia Mazzei and Dariel Pradas
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba speaking in Havana in January.

item.title: ‘A Lot of Life Years Lost’: How NAFTA Shortened American Life Spans
item.description: A study tracks how the North American Free Trade Agreement and trade competition with Mexico led to earlier deaths for American factory workers.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: A steel mill in Braddock, Pa. Researchers found that the North American Free Trade Agreement sped up the decline of the U.S. manufacturing industry.

item.title: Carney Plans Military Expansion in Canada’s Arctic, Following Trump Threats
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada would build three bases in the region. The government also plans to improve infrastructure and airports in the north.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, visited the Royal Canadian Air Force 440 Transport Squadron, in Yellowknife, Northwest Territories, on Thursday.

item.title: Cuba Pledges to Release 51 Prisoners Amid U.S. Pressure
item.description: The Trump administration has been trying to choke the Cuban government through an oil blockade. The prisoner release appears to be an effort to appease Washington.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas, Emiliano Rodríguez Mega and Frances Robles
item.media:description: The Capitol building in Havana.

item.title: Trump Tempers María Corina Machado’s Political Ambitions in Venezuela
item.description: President Trump is tempering the political ambitions of María Corina Machado, a Nobel laureate, as he deepens ties with her foes in Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: María Corina Machado after leaving a meeting with senators outside of the U.S. Capitol in January.

item.title: With New Right-Wing President, Chile Shifts Toward Region’s Conservative, Pro-Trump Alignment
item.description: The inauguration of Chile’s new president, José Antonio Kast, is the latest milestone in a broader shift toward conservatism and pro-Trump alignment in the region.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:description: President José Antonio Kast of Chile and his wife, María Pía Adriasola, at the La Moneda presidential palace in Santiago, Chile, on Wednesday.

item.title: Haitian President’s Widow Recalls Killers Spoke Spanish and Used Nicknames
item.description: The prosecution used testimony by Martine Moïse about the language she heard on the night her husband was killed to support its claim that the assassination was carried out by a hired Colombian hit squad.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martine Moïse, left, and her son in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in 2021, after the attack that killed her husband.

item.title: Two People Die After Paid Plasma Donation at Clinics in Canada
item.description: Grifols, a Spanish health care company, operates clinics in Canada that collect blood plasma from donors in exchange for an honorarium.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Roni Caryn Rabin
item.media:description: The plasma clinics were operated by Grifols, a Spanish health care company.

item.title: Trump Administration Asks Supreme Court to End Protections for Haitian Immigrants
item.description: The administration has also sought to end deportation protections for other immigrant groups, including Syrians.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: A detention center in San Diego. The Trump administration’s filing Wednesday is the second such emergency petition pending before the court dealing with whether it can end the protected status for a group of immigrants.

item.title: U.S. Seeks Extradition of Maduro Ally Alex Saab From Venezuela
item.description: The indicted tycoon Alex Saab is being detained in Venezuela on a U.S. request. His case tests President Trump’s sway over the country’s new rulers.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev, Annie Correal and Mariana Martínez
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: The businessman Alex Saab, center, in Caracas, Venezuela, in 2024.

item.title: Is a Private-Island Paradise in Turks and Caicos Worth the Price? I Went to Find Out.
item.description: What it’s like to stay on a private island so luxurious that there are no price tags and so quiet that “even the wind feels guilty for making noise.”
item.dc:creator: Danial Adkison

item.title: Trump Casts a Shadow Over One of Mexico’s Deadliest States
item.description: President Trump wants to strike cartels inside Mexico. In Sinaloa State, a cartel stronghold, some residents said they were willing to entertain U.S. intervention.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas, Jack Nicas and Adriana Zehbrauskas
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: National Guard soldiers last month in Sinaloa, Mexico.

item.title: Widow Gives Harrowing Testimony About Assassination of Haiti’s President
item.description: Martine Moïse was the first witness called in the long-awaited Miami trial of the people accused of plotting the murder of her husband, Jovenel Moïse, in 2021.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martine Moise, speaking during the funeral of her husband, Jovenel Moise, in Cap Haitien, Haiti, in 2021.

item.title: Woman Found Innocent After 22 Years in Prison Will Not Be Deported
item.description: Carmen Mejia was cleared of murder in connection with the death of a baby in her care, who was scalded by hot water in 2003 in Austin, Texas. Federal officials said she will be allowed to stay in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk and Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Mikala Compton/Austin American-Statesman, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Carmen Mejia entering the courtroom for a hearing in Austin, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: Mother of Tumbler Ridge Shooting Victim Sues OpenAI
item.description: The company banned the shooter’s ChatGPT account but did not alert the authorities, a move that amounted to fatal negligence, the family claims.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Alana Paterson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial for the victims of a mass shooting last month at Tumbler Ridge Secondary School in British Columbia.

item.title: Freezing Rain, Ice and Snow Expected Across Ottawa and Montreal
item.description: Canada’s weather service warned that heavy snow, ice and strong winds could make travel difficult in Ottawa and Montreal.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:description: A shoveler clearing snow in Montreal last month. The area is expected to be hit with heavy snowfall.

item.title: The Killing Of Haiti’s President Heads to a U.S. Court. What to Know.
item.description: Four men are accused in South Florida of plotting to kill President Jovenel Moise in 2021. His death plunged Haiti into chaos that continues today.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.category: Center for Economic and Policy Research
item.media:description: Jovenel Moise, center, in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in 2018. He was killed in his home in 2021.

item.title: Shooting at U.S. Consulate in Toronto a National Security Incident, Police Say
item.description: The police are searching for two men who both fired at the building with a single handgun and fled in a white SUV.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Frank Gunn/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Toronto Police Services said that there was a “firearm discharge” at the U.S. Consulate on Tuesday morning but that there were no injuries.
